title: Boundary regularity for area minimizing currents and a question of Almgren
speaker: Camillo De Lellis
abstract: Abstract: Consider an area minimizing integral current $T$ which has a smooth boundary $\Gamma$ of multiplicity $1$ in some smooth Riemannian ambient manifold $\Sigma$. If the current has codimension $1$ a famous work of Hardt and Simon gives full regularity of $T$ at the boundary. In higher codimension Allard's theory can be used to show regularity at points $p\in \Gamma$ where there is a uniformly convex barrier such that $T$ lies locally on one side of it (cf. Hardt's work).
tags: Workshop on Mean Curvature and Regularity

proof_stages.label: Area-minimizing currents and regular boundary types
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up an integral rectifiable area-minimizing current T in a smooth ambient manifold Sigma with smooth boundary Gamma, then defines interior regular points, one-sided boundary regular points, and two-sided boundary regular points.

proof_stages.label: Known regularity theory and the high-codimension boundary gap
proof_stages.summary: The talk reviews the codimension-one interior and boundary regularity theorems, Almgren-Chang interior theory in higher codimension, and the prior absence of general information on high-codimension boundary singularities.

proof_stages.label: Dense boundary regularity theorem and Almgren's question
proof_stages.summary: The main joint theorem is stated: the boundary singular set is meager, equivalently boundary regular points are dense, and this is related to Allard-Hardt one-sided regularity and to Almgren's question about connected boundaries having one-sided regular points.

proof_stages.label: Accumulating boundary singularities as the obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A smooth curve in R4 with countably many accumulating boundary singularities shows that the boundary problem is harder than the interior theory and that one cannot simply prove a boundary version of isolated-singularity results.

proof_stages.label: Interior singularities as unique continuation
proof_stages.summary: The interior theory is reinterpreted through branch singularities modeled on holomorphic curves and the unique-continuation principle that holomorphic zeros cannot accumulate in the interior.

proof_stages.label: Reduction from boundary nonregularity to zero-boundary unique continuation
proof_stages.summary: The key heuristic is that if boundary singular points had nonempty interior, the problem behaves not like arbitrary boundary zero accumulation but like a unique-continuation problem with zero boundary data.

proof_stages.label: Boundary-adapted frequency monotonicity
proof_stages.summary: Using a harmonic toy problem, the speaker explains the robust frequency-function replacement with a cutoff and an adapted distance whose gradient is tangent to Gamma, yielding monotonicity up to an error term and supplying the main analytic mechanism for the proof.

examples.name: Coalescing same-oriented parallel circles
examples.note: Two equally oriented circles in parallel planes collapse to a minimizer with two sheets on one side and one sheet on the other, illustrating that two-sided boundary regular points can really occur.
examples.engagement_reason: he traces the same-oriented parallel circles through sliding and coalescence, identifying the limiting minimizer and its two-sided boundary multiplicities

examples.name: Smooth curve in R4 with countably many boundary singularities
examples.note: This C-infinity boundary curve whose unique minimizer has boundary singularities accumulating at the origin visibly forces the audience to abandon a direct boundary analogue of Chang's isolated-singularity theorem and motivates the meagerness strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: he discusses the smooth R4 curve example in detail, including countably many boundary singularities clustering at the origin and the tangent-cone/branch-point behavior there

examples.name: Smallest enclosing ball as a convex barrier
examples.note: In Euclidean space the smallest ball containing the boundary gives a touching convex barrier, illustrating how Allard-Hardt one-sided regularity can at least produce a regular boundary point in special global situations.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the smallest ball containing the boundary as a concrete convex barrier and applies Allard-Hardt regularity at its touching point
